retrieved_docs.title: Amantadine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the proper indications for amantadine therapy. Describe the mechanism of action of amantadine. Review the potential adverse effects of amantadine. Outline the importance of collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team and how it can enhance patient care when dosing amantadine to improve patient outcomes for Parkinson patients. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Amantadine -- Administration -- FDA Approved
retrieved_docs.content: Amantadine is typically administered orally once daily or in divided doses depending on the indication. For the treatment of influenza A, amantadine 200 mg daily or 100 mg twice daily should be administered within 24 to 48 hours after the onset of symptoms and should be continued for 24 to 48 hours after the symptoms have resolved. For prophylaxis, patients should continue treatment for the entirety of the influenza season. For the treatment of Parkinson disease, amantadine is also given orally at 100 mg twice daily and increased to 200 mg twice daily as needed. Dosing should begin at 100 mg once daily for patients taking other parkinsonian drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: Amantadine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Amantadine is an antiviral agent with mild antiparkinsonian activity. Amantadine was used in the early 2000s for Influenza A treatment. However, high levels of resistance have emerged recently, initially from Asia and now in North America. As of 2011, the CDC does not recommend treatment or prophylaxis of influenza A due to the high levels of resistance. Amantadine is now used mostly for Parkinson disease. This activity outlines the indications, contraindications, activity, adverse drug reactions, warning, precautions, toxicity, and limitations of amantadine in the clinical setting as relates to the essential points necessary for members of an interprofessional team managing the care of patients with Parkinson disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Amantadine -- Administration -- Non-FDA Approved
retrieved_docs.content: Huntington chorea: 100 mg orally three to four times daily. Multiple sclerosis-related fatigues: 100 mg orally twice daily, increasing to 200 mg twice daily as needed. Restless leg syndrome: 100 mg orally daily, increasing to 300 mg per day as needed. Traumatic brain injury: 100 mg orally twice daily, increasing to 200 mg twice daily as needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Amantadine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Besides the two FDA-approved usages of amantadine, a few other diseases can benefit from amantadine. Clinical trials have conflicting results for the reduction of chorea in Huntington disease. The 2012 American Academy of Neurology guidelines suggests amantadine is likely effective in decreasing chorea, although the degree of effect is unknown. [4] Amantadine is a first-line agent for fatigue in multiple sclerosis. Amantadine may be useful in restless leg syndrome. However, data is limited to a small clinical trial. Amantadine has also been used in traumatic brain injuries. Initial studies had suggested that it may promote functional recovery. A clinical trial in 2012 concluded amantadine accelerated the pace of functional recovery in patients with severe traumatic brain injuries. However, there were no significant differences in overall improvement compared with the placebo group.

retrieved_docs.content: Amantadine, atracurium, azathioprine, diflunisal, dicumarol, imatinib mesylate, saquinavir, and etodolac are all medications that should be avoided in patients with drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية). The presence of a drug allergy constitutes a contraindication for the use of each of these agents, underscoring the need for clinicians to assess hypersensitivity history before prescribing any of these drugs to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Meperidine is classified as a spasmolytic agent, indicating its primary therapeutic use in relieving smooth‑muscle spasms. However, meperidine is contraindicated for use together with selegiline, meaning that co‑administration of these two drugs should be avoided because of the risk of adverse interactions. This relationship underscores the importance of recognizing drug class memberships and contraindications when selecting and combining medications for patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Akamprosát, atrakurium, clorazepate, imatinib mesylát, saquinavir, teriparatid, etodolac, naltrexon and nimodipin are all drugs that should be avoided in patients who have فرط الحساسية الدوائية (drug hypersensitivity), as each is listed as contraindicated in the presence of this condition. Both akamprosát and naltrexon belong to the class of odvykací prostředky alkoholu (alcohol‑withdrawal agents), indicating that they are used to support cessation of alcohol dependence. In addition, nimodipin is specifically contraindicated with the therapeutic class that includes saquinavir, suggesting that co‑administration of nimodipin with saquinavir or related agents is not recommended. This network of contraindications highlights the importance of reviewing a patient’s allergy history and medication class interactions before prescribing any of these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 26
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” comprises agents such as aniracetam, acylcarnitine, adrafinil (a pro‑drug of modafinil and α‑adrenergic agonist), cytidine‑diphosphate‑choline, fipexide, nizofenone, oxiracetam, pipradrol, prolintane, temgicoluril (structurally related to mebikar) and others, classified as psychoanaleptics used for ADHD and cognitive enhancement. Within this group, centrally acting sympathomimetics—including atomoxetine, methamfetamin, methylfenidát and modafinil—are employed for the same indications. Antidepressants form a distinct subclass of psychoanaleptics and are therapeutically linked to the sodium‑valproate salt class; representative members include citalopram, lithium, bupropion (and its hydrobromide and hydrochloride salts, also a smoking‑cessation agent), desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride, esketamine, sidnocarb, sulpiride, atomoxetine, fipexide, iproniazid (also listed under antituberculotics for its historic MAOI activity), klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolapram, tofisopam, methylfenidát, pipradrol and prolintane, with trazodone serving as the prototypical parent concept. Important safety interactions are noted: citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride and trazodone are contraindicated with the oxazolidinone antibiotic linezolid because of serotonin‑syndrome risk; citalopram use may be complicated by alcohol dependence; and bupropion and its salts are contraindicated in patients experiencing withdrawal syndrome, underscoring the need to evaluate dependence‑related conditions before prescribing. Tofisopam is specifically indicated for the treatment of withdrawal syndrome, highlighting its role in managing substance‑discontinuation symptoms. The trade name Antituberkulotika refers to an isoniazid 100 mg/mL injectable solution used for Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection, illustrating the therapeutic application of antitubercular agents such as iproniazid within this context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2281
retrieved_docs.content: Aspirin, amantadin, atrakurium, azathioprin, diflunisal, digoxin, dikumarol, imatinib mesylát, saquinavir, and etodolac are all medications that are contraindicated in patients with drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية). In addition, aspirin and digoxin are reported to co‑occur, suggesting that they may be prescribed together in certain clinical contexts; however, because both drugs share the same contraindication for drug hypersensitivity, clinicians must carefully evaluate a patient’s allergy history before using them concurrently.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 106
retrieved_docs.content: Antidepressants are classified within the broader psychoanaleptics domain and represent a heterogeneous therapeutic class that includes classic serotonergic agents (citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride), norepinephrine‑dopamine reuptake inhibitors (bupropion hydrobromide, bupropion hydrochloride), NMDA‑receptor antagonists (esketamine), mood‑stabilizers (lithium), atypical antipsychotics with antidepressant use (sulpiride), stimulants (methylphenidate, atomoxetine, methylfenidát, modafinil, methamfetamin), and other compounds such as aniracetam, moklobemide, klorgylin, fipexide, deanol, nialamid, rolipram, and tofisofam. Trazodone is identified as a parent concept for antidepressants, and both trazodone and the antibiotic linezolid share a contraindication, while linezolid is also contraindicated with citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, and duloxetine hydrochloride, underscoring the need for careful medication reconciliation. Psychoanaleptics represent a higher‑order class that encompasses both anti‑dementia drugs and the psychostimulant/ADHD/nootropic agents. Anti‑dementia drugs form a subclass of psychoanaleptics, with memantine listed under the ATC group “Other anti‑dementia drugs.” Disorders caused by psychoactive substances include withdrawal syndromes; SSRI withdrawal is a specific subtype. Hydroxyzine hydrochloride may be employed to treat withdrawal syndromes, and tofisopam may be used for SSRI‑withdrawal management. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1967
retrieved_docs.content: Meperidine is contraindicated for use together with selegiline, indicating that these two agents should not be co‑administered because of a class‑based interaction. In addition, fenelzin sulfát is also listed as a contraindicated class for meperidine, meaning that fenelzin sulfát should not be given to patients receiving meperidine. Consequently, meperidine should be avoided in combination with either selegiline or fenelzin sulfát to prevent adverse drug interactions.
